stem: X-rays used in diagnostic imaging are best described as:

opts.C: Visible light with very short wavelength

exp: Diagnostic x-rays are electromagnetic radiation with high frequency and short wavelength. They have no mass or charge. Sound waves are mechanical and require a medium. X-rays are not visible light.

stem: The ALARA principle is best summarized as keeping radiation exposure:

opts.C: As high as needed for perfect images

exp: ALARA means minimizing dose while obtaining adequate diagnostic information. “Perfect images” can drive unnecessary exposure. ALARA applies to all patients. Grids do not eliminate dose and often require higher mAs.

stem: Which factor primarily controls the quantity of x-rays produced?
exp: mAs controls x-ray quantity (number of photons). kVp primarily affects beam energy/penetration and contrast. SID affects intensity at the detector geometrically. Grid ratio affects scatter cleanup and exposure requirements.

stem: A detector artifact is most strongly suggested when an abnormal pattern appears:

opts.B: In the same location on repeated images

exp: Fixed-location patterns across repeats suggest a detector/processing artifact. Patient-only, variable-location, or motion-dependent patterns point away from detector hardware issues.

stem: A routine PA chest radiograph is typically exposed on:

opts.C: Any breathing phase if the patient is upright

exp: Second full inspiration maximizes lung inflation and improves visualization. Expiration is used for specific indications, not routine PA chest. Mid-inspiration and “any phase” increase motion and reduce consistency.

stem: The routine projection for demonstrating phalanges and metacarpals of the hand is most commonly:
exp: PA hand is the standard routine projection for hand anatomy. AP is less common as the routine baseline. Lateral is complementary, not sufficient alone. Tangential is for specific structures.

stem: A patient speaks limited English and appears confused about breath-hold instructions for an upright chest radiograph. What is the best initial action?

opts.A: Continue the exam to avoid delaying the schedule
opts.B: Use simple gestures and demonstrate the instruction
opts.C: Speak louder and repeat the instruction
opts.D: Ask another patient in the room to translate

exp: Demonstration and simple gestures improve understanding while maintaining privacy and professionalism. Speaking louder doesn’t improve comprehension. Proceeding without understanding increases motion risk. Using another patient as an interpreter risks confidentiality and accuracy.

stem: A patient becomes dizzy when standing for a lower leg radiograph. What should the technologist do first?

opts.A: Assist the patient to a seated or supine position and assess responsiveness
opts.B: Leave the room to find help after positioning is complete
opts.C: Ask the patient to “push through it” to avoid repeats
opts.D: Continue the exposure quickly before the patient moves

exp: Patient safety comes first—support and reassess to prevent a fall and determine if assistance is needed. Rushing the exposure increases harm risk. Leaving the patient unattended is unsafe. Encouraging them to continue can worsen syncope.

stem: A technologist doubles their distance from the x-ray source during a portable exposure. Approximately how does their exposure change?
exp: Exposure follows the inverse square law; doubling distance reduces intensity to 1/4. Doubling or halving contradicts inverse-square behavior. One-eighth would correspond to increasing distance by about 2.8×, not 2×.

stem: What is the primary patient-dose benefit of added filtration?

opts.A: Increases beam divergence to cover more anatomy
opts.B: Increases scatter production to improve detector exposure
opts.C: Increases the number of low-energy photons absorbed by the patient
opts.D: Removes low-energy photons that would be absorbed superficially

exp: Filtration removes low-energy photons that contribute to skin dose without improving image formation. Low-energy photons absorbed by the patient increase dose, not decrease it. Divergence is geometric, not filtration-driven. Scatter does not improve image quality.

stem: Increasing kVp (with appropriate mAs adjustment) generally results in:
exp: Higher kVp increases penetration and scatter, producing longer-scale (lower) contrast. Motion blur depends on time and movement, not kVp alone. Magnification depends on geometry (OID/SID).

stem: A radiograph shows multiple small, bright circular spots in the same location on repeated images. What is the most likely cause?

opts.C: Dust/debris on the detector or protective cover

exp: Fixed-location spots across repeats strongly suggest detector/cover contamination or a detector artifact. Motion changes position between exposures. Low kVp affects overall appearance/noise, not fixed spots. Grid misalignment produces density variation patterns, not discrete fixed circles.

stem: For an upright chest radiograph, the best time to expose to demonstrate maximum lung expansion is at:
exp: End of the second deep inspiration typically provides maximal lung inflation and lowers the diaphragm. End expiration is used for some special evaluations but not routine maximum expansion. Beginning or normal breathing increases motion risk.

stem: For a PA wrist radiograph, which positioning best demonstrates the wrist joint spaces?

opts.B: Wrist rotated into a lateral position
opts.C: Hand pronated with wrist and hand in the same plane

exp: A true PA wrist requires the hand pronated with the wrist/hand in the same plane to open joint spaces. Flexion and curled fingers alter alignment. Elbow elevation introduces distortion. A lateral is a different projection.

stem: In an x-ray tube, the primary function of the filament is to:
exp: Heating the filament releases electrons via thermionic emission. Intensifying screens convert x-rays to light, not the filament. Photons are not accelerated in the tube—electrons are. Scatter absorption is a grid function.

stem: During a portable exam, which action most effectively reduces technologist exposure?

opts.A: Hold the detector to prevent motion
opts.B: Stand closer to reduce scatter angle
opts.C: Maximize distance and use protective barriers when available
opts.D: Increase field size to reduce repeats

exp: Distance and shielding are highly effective for reducing exposure. Standing closer increases exposure. Increasing field size increases scatter. Holding the detector is unsafe and increases occupational dose.

stem: To reduce magnification on a radiograph, the technologist should primarily:
exp: Lower OID reduces magnification. Increasing OID increases magnification. Decreasing SID increases magnification. Exposure time affects motion, not magnification.

stem: The primary purpose of a light field–radiation field congruence test is to verify:
exp: Congruence testing confirms the collimator light field matches the actual x-ray field. Focal spot size and kVp waveform are separate QC areas. Grid ratio is a physical grid property, not a collimator alignment test.

stem: To best demonstrate ribs above the diaphragm, exposures are typically made on:
exp: Inspiration elevates the rib cage and lowers the diaphragm, improving visualization of upper ribs. Expiration is often used for ribs below the diaphragm. Shallow breathing risks motion. Valsalva is not standard for rib imaging.

stem: To best open the ankle mortise joint space, the technologist should position the ankle in:

opts.B: Medial rotation of the entire leg 15–20 degrees
opts.D: External rotation of the foot 45 degrees

exp: Ankle mortise is best demonstrated with 15–20° medial rotation to align the malleoli parallel to the detector. Lateral is a different projection. Plantar flexion doesn’t open the mortise. Large external rotation misaligns the joint.

stem: Before beginning a non-emergent radiographic exam, the technologist should first:

opts.A: Explain the exam and verify patient identity using two identifiers
opts.B: Ask the patient to sign a form without discussion
opts.C: Rely on the requisition name only if the patient is cooperative
opts.D: Position the patient to save time and explain afterward

exp: Proper identification and clear explanation support safety and informed participation. Positioning first can lead to errors and anxiety. A signature alone is not informed consent. A requisition name alone is insufficient identification.

stem: The AP axial (Towne) skull projection is primarily used to demonstrate the:

opts.D: Occipital bone and foramen magnum region

exp: The Towne projection emphasizes the occipital bone and foramen magnum region. Lateral sella is a lateral skull. Parietals are better assessed on other skull projections. Nasal bones require dedicated nasal projections.

stem: A patient develops mild urticaria immediately after IV iodinated contrast administration. What is the most appropriate initial response by the radiographer?
exp: Mild contrast reactions (e.g., urticaria, pruritus) require monitoring and physician notification. Epinephrine is reserved for severe reactions.

stem: In the chain of infection, which component represents the method by which a pathogen is transferred from one host to another?
exp: Mode of transmission refers to the mechanism of spread (direct contact, droplet, airborne, etc.).

stem: When transferring a patient from stretcher to table using a transfer board, which principle reduces risk of injury?
exp: A wide base of support improves stability and reduces musculoskeletal strain.

stem: Bremsstrahlung radiation is produced when an electron:
exp: Bremsstrahlung results from electron deceleration in the nuclear field.

stem: The principle of ALARA applies primarily to:
exp: ALARA specifically addresses minimizing occupational exposure.

stem: The minimum lead equivalent for protective aprons is typically:
exp: Standard protective aprons are 0.5 mm lead equivalent.

stem: A dosimeter should be worn:
exp: Collar placement monitors exposure to head and thyroid.

stem: Increasing mAs primarily affects:
exp: mAs controls quantity of photons reaching receptor.

stem: The anode heel effect results in greater intensity on the:
exp: Beam intensity is greater on cathode side.

stem: Full inspiration chest radiograph demonstrates how many posterior ribs?
exp: 10 posterior ribs indicate adequate inspiration.

stem: The AP axial foot requires approximately:
exp: Standard AP axial foot is 10° cephalad.

stem: When communicating with a patient who has limited English proficiency, the most appropriate action is to:

opts.C: Ask a family member to interpret

exp: A qualified interpreter ensures accuracy and protects patient rights and safety.

stem: Standard precautions require hand hygiene:

opts.B: Only when gloves are visibly soiled

exp: Hand hygiene is required before and after all patient contact.

stem: If a patient becomes pulseless in the imaging room, the radiographer’s first action is to:

opts.D: Call a code and begin CPR

exp: Activate emergency response and begin CPR immediately.

stem: When moving a patient, twisting while lifting increases risk of:
exp: Twisting under load increases musculoskeletal injury risk.

stem: Characteristic radiation occurs when an electron:
exp: Inner-shell ionization followed by outer-shell transition produces characteristic x-rays.

stem: Doubling fluoroscopy time while keeping all other factors constant will:
exp: Dose is directly proportional to time.

stem: Scatter radiation increases with:
exp: Higher kVp increases Compton interactions and scatter production.

stem: Increasing object-to-image distance causes:
exp: Greater OID increases magnification and distortion.

stem: Collimation primarily reduces:
exp: Collimation reduces scatter reaching the detector, improving contrast.

stem: Voluntary motion artifact primarily reduces:
exp: Motion blurs detail, reducing recorded sharpness.

stem: A Hoyer lift is primarily used to:
exp: Mechanical lifts reduce staff injury risk during transfers.

stem: Added filtration primarily removes:
exp: Filtration removes low-energy photons that contribute to skin dose.

stem: Reducing field size will:
exp: Smaller field size reduces scatter production.

stem: Contrast resolution refers to the ability to:
exp: Contrast resolution differentiates subtle tissue density differences.

stem: Supine abdomen CR is centered at:
exp: Iliac crest approximates L4-L5 for abdomen centering.

stem: Medial oblique foot best demonstrates:
exp: Oblique foot demonstrates cuboid and sinus tarsi region.

stem: Sterile technique differs from medical asepsis because it:
exp: Sterile technique aims to eliminate all microorganisms in a sterile field.

stem: A living will primarily addresses:
exp: Living wills document end-of-life care preferences.

stem: Avoiding eye contact during patient interaction may be interpreted as:
exp: Nonverbal cues affect patient trust and comfort.

stem: Increasing tissue thickness results in:
exp: Thicker tissue attenuates more photons.

stem: The fluoroscopy cumulative timer alerts the operator every:
exp: Fluoroscopy cumulative timer provides an audible signal at 5-minute intervals.

stem: Digital systems have wide dynamic range, meaning they:
exp: Wide dynamic range allows acceptable image appearance across a range of exposures.

stem: Digital imaging latitude refers to:
exp: Latitude is the range of exposures that produce acceptable images.

stem: AP femur requires inclusion of:
exp: Include the entire femur with both adjacent joints.

stem: When imaging a young child, the most effective communication technique is to:
exp: Age-appropriate communication reduces anxiety and improves cooperation.

stem: A nasal cannula typically delivers oxygen at a maximum flow rate of approximately:
exp: Nasal cannula typically ranges 1–6 L/min.

stem: Increasing mAs primarily affects beam:
exp: mAs controls the number of photons produced (beam quantity).

stem: Proper collimation reduces patient dose by:
exp: Collimation reduces exposed tissue volume and scatter.

stem: Increasing SID reduces magnification because it:
exp: Greater SID reduces divergence at receptor, reducing magnification.

stem: Lateral foot requires CR centered at:
exp: Center at base of the 3rd metatarsal.

stem: When activating a Code Blue, the radiographer should provide:
exp: Provide clear location and emergency type to mobilize response quickly.

stem: The rotating anode primarily functions to:
exp: Rotation spreads heat across a larger anode surface area.

stem: Protective lead aprons should be stored:
exp: Hanging prevents cracks in shielding from folding.

stem: Personnel dosimeters are typically exchanged:
exp: Dosimeters are commonly exchanged monthly.

stem: Dose reduction in pediatric imaging is most effectively achieved by:

opts.D: Increasing kVp and reducing mAs appropriately

exp: Technique optimization and collimation are key for pediatrics.

stem: Electronic noise differs from quantum noise because it originates from:
exp: Electronic noise originates within system electronics.

stem: Proper collimation improves image quality by reducing:
exp: Less scatter improves contrast.

stem: Nasal bones are best demonstrated in:
exp: Lateral nasal bones are standard for evaluation.

stem: PA hand requires CR centered at:
exp: Center at the 3rd MCP joint for PA hand.

stem: PA finger CR centered at:
exp: Center at the MCP joint for finger imaging.

stem: AP toes CR centered at:
exp: Center at the base of the 1st MTP joint for toes.

stem: AP humerus requires inclusion of:
exp: Include both adjacent joints when imaging long bones.

stem: AP tibia/fibula requires inclusion of:
exp: Include both adjacent joints for tibia/fibula.

stem: AP forearm requires inclusion of:
exp: Include both adjacent joints for forearm.

stem: A patient refuses a radiographic exam after the procedure is explained. What is the radiographer’s best next action?

opts.A: Notify the nurse/provider and document the refusal
opts.B: Ask the family to sign consent for the patient
opts.C: Delay and repeat the explanation until the patient agrees
opts.D: Proceed because the order is in the chart

exp: Patients have the right to refuse; the radiographer should notify the appropriate provider and document the refusal.

stem: The most effective single measure to prevent healthcare-associated infections is:

opts.B: Wearing a mask for all patients
opts.C: Hand hygiene before and after patient contact
opts.D: Using sterile technique for all radiography

exp: Hand hygiene is the cornerstone of Standard Precautions and has the greatest impact on infection prevention.

stem: A “DNR” order primarily affects which aspect of patient care?

opts.B: Whether the exam can be performed
opts.D: Whether CPR is initiated during arrest

exp: DNR specifies limits on resuscitative efforts (e.g., CPR) during cardiac/respiratory arrest.

stem: According to the inverse square law, doubling the distance from a point source changes intensity to:
exp: Intensity is inversely proportional to distance squared; doubling distance reduces intensity to 1/4.

stem: If SID is doubled, the intensity at the image receptor changes by what factor (all else equal)?
exp: Inverse square law: intensity ∝ 1/(distance^2). Doubling distance reduces intensity to 1/4.

stem: Which factor primarily determines x-ray beam quality?
exp: kVp predominantly controls beam quality (average energy/penetration).

stem: Which protective device is specifically used to cover the Bucky slot during fluoroscopy to reduce scatter to personnel?
exp: The Bucky slot cover reduces scatter escaping from the table-side opening during fluoroscopy.

stem: A technologist should stand where during mobile radiography to reduce exposure?

opts.B: Next to the patient’s head, in line with the beam
opts.C: At least 6 feet away and at right angles to the primary beam when possible
opts.D: Directly behind the x-ray tube housing

exp: Distance and positioning at right angles reduce scatter exposure; follow facility policy.

stem: Which post-processing tool changes grayscale mapping for the displayed image without changing raw exposure?
exp: LUT/VOI adjustments change display grayscale without changing exposure that created the raw data.

stem: Which factor MOST directly affects spatial resolution in digital imaging?
exp: Smaller pixels/higher sampling frequency generally improve spatial resolution.

stem: Which component limits exposure time during AEC if the detector fails to terminate exposure?
exp: Back-up timer is a safety device to prevent excessive exposure if AEC fails.

stem: A QC test comparing the light field to the radiation field evaluates:

opts.C: Collimator light field to x-ray field alignment

exp: Beam restriction QC includes verifying light field/radiation field congruence.

stem: For an AP axial sacrum, the central ray is directed:

opts.B: Cephalad to the sacrum to project it free of the pubic symphysis
opts.D: Caudad to the sacrum to magnify it

exp: AP axial sacrum commonly uses cephalad angulation to project sacrum above the pubic symphysis.

stem: For a PA chest radiograph, the shoulders should be rolled forward primarily to:

opts.B: Move scapulae laterally off the lung fields
opts.D: Increase OID to magnify the heart

exp: Rolling shoulders forward moves scapulae laterally, improving visualization of lungs.

stem: In a double-contrast barium enema, the purpose of adding air after barium is to:

opts.D: Distend the colon and coat mucosa for improved detail

exp: Air distends the colon and enhances mucosal coating/detail with barium.

stem: For a lateral sternum, which positioning helps move the sternum off the thoracic spine?

opts.A: PA prone to magnify the sternum
opts.B: LAO positioning to project sternum over the spine
opts.C: RAO positioning (slight obliquity) to project sternum into the heart shadow

exp: Slight RAO helps project sternum over the heart shadow and away from the spine.

stem: For an AP knee, which landmark is commonly used for centering?

opts.A: About 1/2 inch (1–2 cm) inferior to the patellar apex

exp: AP knee is centered near the knee joint; commonly just inferior to patellar apex per protocol.

stem: For a mortise ankle projection, the leg/foot is rotated until the malleoli are:

opts.A: Parallel to the IR, opening the ankle joint space
opts.D: Perpendicular to the IR, closing the joint

exp: Mortise view is achieved with internal rotation to align malleoli parallel and open joint space.

stem: A portable chest radiograph is requested for a patient on contact precautions. Which action best reduces cross-contamination?

opts.A: Wear sterile gloves and reuse them for the next patient
opts.B: Place the detector directly on the bed linens
opts.C: Use two clean detector covers, removing the outer cover before leaving the room
opts.D: Disinfect the detector only after completing all morning portables

exp: Double-bagging allows the contaminated outer cover to be removed inside the room, protecting the detector and environment. Placing the detector on linens increases contamination risk. Gloves are not reused between patients. Delayed disinfection spreads pathogens.

stem: In diagnostic energy ranges, which interaction most contributes to scatter that degrades image contrast?

opts.B: Coherent scattering as the dominant interaction

exp: Compton scattering produces scattered photons that reach the detector and reduce contrast. Photoelectric absorption contributes to subject contrast but does not create scatter that exits the patient. Pair production does not occur at diagnostic energies. Coherent scatter occurs but is not the dominant contributor in most diagnostic exams.

stem: Tight collimation primarily improves image quality by reducing:
exp: Collimation reduces irradiated tissue volume, decreasing scatter and improving contrast. Heel effect depends on anode geometry and field placement. Detector sensitivity is not changed by collimation. Motion blur relates to time and movement.

stem: Which change most directly improves recorded detail on a radiograph?
exp: A smaller focal spot reduces geometric unsharpness and improves spatial resolution. Increasing OID worsens magnification and unsharpness. Decreasing SID increases magnification/unsharpness. Increasing field size increases scatter and can reduce detail visibility.

stem: In fluoroscopy, the feature that automatically adjusts exposure to maintain image brightness is most directly associated with:
exp: Automatic brightness control adjusts technical factors to maintain consistent image brightness. Collimation and filtration affect dose and scatter but do not automatically regulate brightness by themselves. Heel effect is geometric and not a fluoroscopic control system.

stem: A trauma patient arrives with a cervical collar in place for suspected C-spine injury. What is the best approach for positioning?

opts.A: Proceed with routine obliques requiring neck rotation
opts.B: Maintain immobilization and follow trauma imaging protocols
opts.C: Remove the collar to obtain better neck extension
opts.D: Ask the patient to rotate the head to open intervertebral spaces

exp: Suspected cervical injury requires maintaining immobilization and using trauma-appropriate projections/protocols. Removing the collar or rotating the head risks worsening injury. Routine obliques are not appropriate if motion/rotation is unsafe.

stem: On an AP knee radiograph, the fibular head is almost completely superimposed by the tibia. This indicates the knee is most likely:
exp: Excessive superimposition indicates medial (internal) rotation beyond correct AP alignment. Under-rotation shows the fibular head more free. Flexion affects joint space appearance but not this rotation sign.

stem: Increasing patient thickness most directly increases the amount of scatter produced because it:

opts.A: Increases the volume of tissue irradiated

exp: More tissue volume in the beam increases the number of interactions and thus scatter production. Interactions increase, not decrease. Photoelectric absorption still occurs. Beam energy is set by kVp, not by patient thickness.

stem: Which change increases x-ray beam penetrability while generally reducing patient skin dose from low-energy photons?
exp: Added filtration removes low-energy photons and increases average beam energy (beam quality). Decreasing filtration does the opposite. Lowering kVp reduces penetrability. Increasing mAs increases quantity, not penetrability, and can increase dose.

stem: For a routine abdomen radiograph, tight collimation primarily reduces:
exp: Collimation reduces irradiated tissue volume, lowering scatter and improving contrast. Motion, magnification, and focal spot blur are controlled by time and geometry, not field size.

stem: A lateral lumbar image shows low contrast due to excessive scatter. Which change most directly improves contrast?

opts.C: Add a grid or increase beam restriction appropriately

exp: Scatter reduction (beam restriction and/or grid use) improves contrast. Increasing field size increases scatter. SID and time don’t address scatter-driven contrast loss.

stem: A chest radiograph shows motion blur. Which change most directly reduces motion blur while maintaining exposure?

opts.B: Decrease exposure time and increase mA proportionally
opts.C: Increase exposure time and decrease mA
opts.D: Decrease kVp and keep mAs constant

exp: Shorter exposure time reduces motion; increasing mA maintains mAs and receptor exposure. Longer time worsens motion. kVp changes affect contrast/penetration and may alter exposure. SID changes can reduce intensity at the detector without addressing motion cause.

stem: During fluoroscopy, image brightness remains stable as patient thickness increases. Which system is primarily responsible?
exp: Automatic brightness control adjusts technical factors to maintain brightness. Collimation and filtration influence dose/quality but do not automatically maintain brightness. AEC is typically associated with radiographic exposures, not fluoroscopic brightness regulation.

stem: A patient cannot stand for an acute abdomen evaluation. Which position best substitutes for an upright abdomen to demonstrate air-fluid levels?

opts.B: Left lateral decubitus with a horizontal beam

exp: A decubitus with a horizontal beam demonstrates air-fluid levels when upright is not possible. Supine/prone alone may miss levels. Trendelenburg is not a standard substitute and can be poorly tolerated.

stem: A lateral elbow radiograph shows an elevated posterior fat pad. This finding most strongly suggests:

opts.A: Joint effusion possibly associated with occult fracture

exp: A visible/elevated posterior fat pad is abnormal and commonly indicates effusion, often due to fracture. It’s not a normal positioning marker. Exposure errors don’t create fat pad signs. Motion produces blur.

stem: A patient with severe shoulder pain cannot abduct for a routine axillary view. What is the best approach?

opts.A: Skip the lateral entirely and repeat the AP with higher mAs
opts.B: Ask the ordering provider to cancel the lateral projection
opts.C: Force abduction briefly and expose quickly
opts.D: Use an alternative trauma lateral projection that minimizes movement

exp: When movement is limited, use an alternative lateral that meets the clinical goal while minimizing pain and risk. Forcing motion is unsafe. Canceling without consultation may compromise diagnosis. Repeating the AP doesn’t replace orthogonal information.

stem: Lumbar spine obliques are primarily performed to demonstrate the:
exp: Lumbar obliques best show zygapophyseal joints. Cervical obliques emphasize foramina, but lumbar foramina are best on lateral. Spinous processes are seen on AP. SI joints are evaluated with dedicated SI views.

stem: A trauma patient is unconscious and requires an emergent CT scan with contrast. No family is present. What type of consent applies?
exp: Implied consent applies in life-threatening emergencies when immediate treatment is required.

stem: A patient becomes diaphoretic and confused prior to an exam. They state they are diabetic. What should the radiographer suspect first?
exp: Diaphoresis and confusion are classic signs of hypoglycemia.

stem: A patient’s eGFR is significantly reduced. What is the primary concern before administering iodinated contrast?
exp: Reduced eGFR indicates increased risk of contrast-induced nephropathy.

stem: If distance from the source is doubled, radiation intensity becomes:
exp: Intensity is inversely proportional to distance squared.

stem: Increasing source-to-skin distance during fluoroscopy will:
exp: Greater source-to-skin distance reduces skin entrance dose.

stem: Compton interactions primarily affect:
exp: Compton scatter increases fog and reduces image contrast.

stem: Deterministic effects are characterized by:
exp: Deterministic effects have a threshold and severity increases with dose.

stem: Increasing kVp while maintaining mAs will:
exp: Higher kVp increases beam penetration and reduces contrast.

stem: Increasing SID while maintaining exposure factors will:
exp: Greater SID reduces magnification.

stem: Using a grid primarily improves:
exp: Grids absorb scatter and improve contrast.

stem: Increasing exposure to the detector reduces:
exp: Higher exposure improves signal-to-noise ratio.

stem: Incorrect centering over AEC chamber results in:
exp: If anatomy does not cover the active cell properly, exposure errors occur.

stem: Electronic cropping after exposure primarily affects:
exp: Cropping hides anatomy but does not reduce dose; metadata/processing integrity is affected.

stem: For a trauma lateral C-spine, the central ray is directed:
exp: Trauma lateral requires horizontal beam to maintain immobilization.

stem: The AP axial cervical spine requires what CR angle?
exp: Standard AP axial C-spine uses 15–20° cephalad.

stem: An upright abdomen is performed primarily to evaluate:
exp: Upright abdomen demonstrates free air and air-fluid levels.

stem: The Grashey method best demonstrates:
exp: Grashey shows true AP glenoid joint space.

stem: The Holmblad method demonstrates:
exp: Holmblad visualizes intercondylar fossa.

stem: Posterior oblique lumbar spine demonstrates:
exp: Posterior obliques show zygapophyseal joints.

stem: The Judet method evaluates:
exp: Judet views evaluate acetabular columns.

stem: To demonstrate left posterior ribs, position patient in:
exp: Posterior ribs are demonstrated on side closest to IR (RPO for left posterior).

stem: Discussing a patient’s exam results in an elevator with coworkers violates:
exp: HIPAA protects patient confidentiality in all settings.

stem: During contrast injection, swelling and pain occur at the IV site. The radiographer should first:
exp: Stop the injection immediately to limit tissue damage; then follow facility protocol.

stem: The half-value layer represents the:

opts.A: Beam filtration thickness reducing intensity by half

exp: HVL is the thickness of material that reduces beam intensity by 50%, reflecting beam quality.

stem: Increasing distance from 1 m to 3 m reduces exposure to approximately:
exp: Inverse square law: (1/3)^2 = 1/9.

stem: Stochastic effects are characterized by:
exp: Stochastic effects have no threshold; probability increases with dose.

stem: Automatic brightness control compensates for increased patient thickness by:
exp: ABC increases x-ray output to maintain image brightness.

stem: A declared pregnant technologist should wear fetal dosimeter:
exp: Fetal badge is worn under the apron at waist level to estimate fetal exposure.

stem: A smaller focal spot improves:
exp: Smaller focal spot reduces geometric unsharpness.

stem: Increasing bit depth primarily increases:
exp: Greater bit depth provides more grayscale levels (better contrast resolution).

stem: Increasing kVp by 15% while halving mAs results in:
exp: The 15% rule maintains receptor exposure when mAs is halved.

stem: For femur imaging, thicker portion should be placed under:
exp: Cathode side has greater x-ray intensity due to heel effect.

stem: Off-level grid alignment causes:
exp: Off-level grids absorb primary beam causing cutoff.

stem: For AP thoracic spine, CR is directed:
exp: Standard AP thoracic spine uses a perpendicular CR.

stem: AP axial L5-S1 requires:
exp: L5-S1 requires caudad angle to open the disc space.

stem: An AP lordotic chest best demonstrates:
exp: Lordotic projects clavicles above lung apices.

stem: A left lateral decubitus abdomen demonstrates:
exp: Left lateral decubitus positions right side up so free air rises and is seen adjacent to liver.

stem: A true lateral elbow demonstrates:
exp: A true lateral elbow shows superimposed humeral epicondyles.

stem: PA wrist with ulnar deviation best demonstrates:
exp: Ulnar deviation elongates the scaphoid.

stem: Cross-table lateral hip CR is directed:
exp: CR is perpendicular to the femoral neck for lateral hip.

stem: Posterior oblique SI joints demonstrate:
exp: Posterior obliques demonstrate the SI joint closest to the IR (side down).

stem: Plantodorsal axial calcaneus requires CR angle of approximately:
exp: Calcaneus axial uses ~40° cephalad angle.

stem: Axiolateral oblique mandible demonstrates:
exp: Axiolateral oblique can demonstrate mandibular body (depending on positioning).

stem: A patient with active tuberculosis requires which isolation precaution?
exp: Tuberculosis requires airborne precautions due to aerosolized transmission.

stem: Compared with ionic contrast agents, nonionic contrast media are generally associated with:
exp: Nonionic contrast has lower osmolality and fewer adverse reactions.

stem: When explaining a procedure to a patient with mild dementia, the most effective strategy is to:
exp: Simple, clear instructions improve comprehension.

stem: The doctrine of respondeat superior means the:

opts.C: Employer may be liable for employee actions

exp: Employers may be held responsible for actions of employees.

stem: During a seizure in the imaging room, the radiographer should first:
exp: Protect patient from injury; do not restrain forcefully or insert objects into mouth.

stem: Air kerma measures:
exp: Air kerma refers to kinetic energy released per unit mass in air.

stem: Effective dose accounts for:
exp: Effective dose accounts for varying tissue sensitivities (tissue weighting).

stem: Cells are more radiosensitive in the presence of:
exp: Oxygen enhances radiation damage and radiosensitivity.

stem: High-level control fluoroscopy results in:
exp: High-level control increases output for difficult imaging, increasing dose rate.

stem: Annual occupational effective dose limit for radiation workers is approximately:
exp: The annual occupational effective dose limit is 50 mSv (5 rem).

stem: Photoelectric interactions are more likely with:
exp: Photoelectric probability increases with lower kVp and higher atomic number materials.

stem: Increasing matrix size while maintaining field of view will:
exp: More pixels per area improves spatial resolution.

stem: Quantum mottle is primarily caused by:
exp: Low photon counts increase quantum noise.

stem: Smaller detector elements result in:
exp: Smaller detector elements improve spatial resolution.

stem: A high exposure indicator number in DR typically suggests:
exp: Higher exposure indicators generally correspond to higher detector exposure (vendor-dependent).

stem: Increasing SID requires what adjustment to maintain receptor exposure?
exp: Increasing distance reduces intensity; increase mAs to maintain exposure.

stem: A wedge filter is used primarily to:
exp: Compensating filters equalize receptor exposure for uneven body parts.

stem: Incorrect histogram analysis in CR most often results in:
exp: Histogram errors cause processing mistakes affecting brightness/contrast.

stem: The open-mouth odontoid view demonstrates:

opts.B: Dens and lateral masses of C1

exp: Odontoid view visualizes dens and C1 lateral masses.

stem: Lateral thoracic spine often uses breathing technique to:
exp: Breathing blurs superimposed ribs and lung markings.

stem: Sternum is best demonstrated in:
exp: RAO projects sternum over heart shadow.

stem: Scapular Y view is used to assess:
exp: Scapular Y is used to evaluate shoulder dislocation.

stem: Merchant method demonstrates:
exp: Merchant demonstrates patellofemoral joint.

stem: AP lumbar spine requires CR centered at:
exp: AP lumbar spine typically centered at L3.

stem: For right anterior ribs, patient should be positioned in:
exp: Anterior ribs are demonstrated on side away from IR; LAO for right anterior ribs.

stem: Waters view best demonstrates:
exp: Waters best demonstrates maxillary sinuses.

stem: Contrast media dose based on patient weight is primarily used to:
exp: Weight-based dosing balances diagnostic efficacy with safety.

stem: Severe bronchospasm and hypotension following contrast injection indicate:
exp: Airway compromise and hypotension indicate anaphylaxis.

stem: A sudden drop in blood pressure during a procedure may indicate:
exp: Hypotension can indicate shock or severe reaction; treat as emergency.

stem: High linear energy transfer (LET) radiation causes:
exp: High LET deposits energy densely, producing more biologic damage.

stem: Using a grid generally requires:
exp: Grids absorb some primary photons so mAs must increase.

stem: During mobile radiography, personnel should stand at least:
exp: Distance reduces scatter exposure; 6 feet is a common minimum guideline.

stem: The GI syndrome occurs at doses approximately above:
exp: GI syndrome occurs at high whole-body doses (~6 Gy and above).

stem: Increasing filtration will:
exp: Filtration removes low-energy photons, increasing average energy.

stem: The SI unit for absorbed dose is:
exp: Absorbed dose is measured in Gray (Gy).

stem: Geometric unsharpness increases with:
exp: Greater OID increases geometric blur.

stem: High detective quantum efficiency (DQE) means:

opts.C: Better signal detection at lower dose

exp: Higher DQE improves efficiency, enabling lower dose for similar image quality.

stem: Tube angulation without receptor alignment causes:
exp: Improper alignment produces shape distortion.

stem: Increasing grid ratio improves contrast but also:
exp: Higher grid ratios require increased exposure (mAs).

stem: In CR, higher sampling frequency improves:
exp: Higher sampling frequency captures more detail.

stem: Adjusting region of interest (ROI) affects:
exp: ROI/post-processing changes appearance, not patient dose.

stem: Swimmer’s lateral view is performed to visualize:
exp: Swimmer’s view demonstrates the cervicothoracic junction (C7-T1).

stem: For upper thoracic lateral, arms should be:
exp: Arms elevated removes humeri from the thoracic spine field.

stem: Free intraperitoneal air is best seen on:
exp: Free air rises under the diaphragm on upright images.

stem: Bilateral AC joint imaging with weights evaluates:
exp: Weighted AC joints evaluate separation.

stem: Radial head-capitulum view requires CR angled:
exp: Radial head-capitulum uses ~45° cephalad angle.

stem: AP axial sacrum requires CR angled:
exp: AP axial sacrum uses ~15° cephalad angle.

stem: Caldwell sinus view demonstrates primarily:
exp: Caldwell view demonstrates frontal and ethmoid sinuses.

stem: Tangential carpal canal view demonstrates:
exp: Carpal canal view demonstrates the hamate hook.

stem: Lateral lumbar spine requires CR centered at:
exp: Lateral lumbar spine is typically centered at L3.

stem: A healthcare-associated infection acquired during hospitalization is referred to as:
exp: Nosocomial infections originate within healthcare settings.

stem: Barium sulfate is contraindicated in patients with suspected:
exp: Barium is contraindicated with suspected perforation; use water-soluble contrast.

stem: Performing a procedure without patient consent may legally be considered:
exp: Battery is unauthorized physical contact without consent.

stem: Pallor, bradycardia, and hypotension during venipuncture most likely indicate:
exp: Vasovagal reactions commonly cause pallor, bradycardia, and hypotension.

stem: Increasing photon frequency results in:
exp: Energy is directly proportional to frequency.

stem: Gonadal shielding is most appropriate when the gonads are:
exp: Shielding may be used when gonads are close to the primary beam and will not obscure anatomy.

stem: Annual effective dose limit for the general public is approximately:
exp: Public annual effective dose limit is 1 mSv.

stem: Cells are most radiosensitive during which phase?
exp: Cells are most radiosensitive during mitosis (M phase).

stem: Activating magnification mode during fluoroscopy will:
exp: Magnification mode increases dose rate.

stem: Remnant radiation refers to photons that:

opts.B: Exit the patient and reach detector

exp: Remnant radiation is the exiting beam that forms the image.

stem: Reducing kVp while maintaining mAs will generally:
exp: Lower kVp increases radiographic contrast.

stem: Off-center grid alignment causes:
exp: Off-center alignment can produce grid cutoff.

stem: A 12-bit system can display how many gray levels?
exp: 2^12 = 4096 grayscale levels.

stem: Increasing photon quantity improves SNR because it:
exp: More photons reduce quantum noise and improve SNR.

stem: Increasing filtration while keeping mAs constant will:
exp: Filtration removes low-energy photons that add skin dose.

stem: Excessive collimation that cuts off anatomy is an example of:
exp: Collimation that excludes anatomy is an imaging artifact and unacceptable practice.

stem: Flat-field calibration in DR primarily corrects for:
exp: Flat-field calibration corrects detector non-uniformity and pixel defects.

stem: For AP open-mouth view, what must be aligned with skull base?
exp: Align the occlusal plane to avoid superimposition of dens by skull base.

stem: AP thoracic spine is centered at approximately:
exp: T7 corresponds to the inferior angle of the scapula.

stem: To demonstrate left free intraperitoneal air in a patient unable to stand, perform:
exp: Right lateral decubitus places left side up; free air rises and is seen under left abdominal wall.

stem: For right anterior ribs, exposure should be made on:
exp: Anterior ribs are best demonstrated on expiration.

stem: Neer method is used to demonstrate:
exp: Neer demonstrates supraspinatus outlet for impingement evaluation.

stem: Camp Coventry method requires CR angled approximately:
exp: Camp Coventry uses 40–50° caudad angle for intercondylar fossa.

stem: AP axial coccyx requires CR angled:
exp: Coccyx AP axial uses ~10° caudad angle.

stem: Axiolateral inferosuperior (Clements-Nakayama) view is used for:
exp: Clements-Nakayama is used for trauma hip without moving the limb.

stem: Sphenoid sinuses are best demonstrated on:
exp: Lateral demonstrates sphenoid sinus air-fluid levels.

stem: For negligence to be proven, which element must exist?
exp: Negligence requires duty owed, breach, causation, and damages.

stem: A patient taking metformin with reduced renal function should:
exp: Metformin may be held per protocol to reduce risk of lactic acidosis when renal function is impaired after contrast.

stem: Tungsten is used as an anode target because of its:

opts.D: High atomic number and high melting point

exp: Tungsten’s high Z and high melting point are ideal for x-ray production.

stem: The time between radiation exposure and symptom appearance is called:
exp: Latent period occurs between exposure and symptom onset.

stem: Increasing kVp while keeping mAs constant will:
exp: Higher kVp increases beam output/intensity.

stem: Dose equivalent accounts for:
exp: Equivalent dose uses radiation weighting factor to reflect biologic effect.

stem: If a technologist steps from 2 ft to 4 ft from source, exposure becomes:
exp: Distance doubled → intensity quartered (inverse square law).

stem: Annual dose limit for lens of eye is approximately:
exp: Traditional U.S. lens dose limit is 150 mSv; follow facility policy.

stem: Beam hardening occurs when:
exp: Removing low-energy photons increases average beam energy (hardening).

stem: Increasing focal spot size will:
exp: Larger focal spot increases penumbra/blur.

stem: Exposure indicators vary among manufacturers, meaning:
exp: Exposure indicator scales vary; interpret using vendor guidance.

stem: Excessive collimation may cause histogram shift leading to:
exp: Improper histogram analysis from poor collimation can produce underexposed appearance.

stem: Increasing SID from 40 to 80 inches requires mAs change by factor of:
exp: Doubling SID requires mAs x4 to maintain receptor exposure.

stem: Exposure creep occurs because digital systems:
exp: Wide latitude can mask overexposure, leading to gradual dose increases.

stem: Moiré pattern in CR results from:
exp: Moiré is caused by grid frequency interacting with reader sampling.

stem: Smaller pixel pitch results in:
exp: Smaller pixel pitch increases spatial resolution.

stem: Increasing post-processing brightness does not change:
exp: Post-processing changes display, not patient exposure.

stem: PA axial (Caldwell) skull requires CR angled:
exp: Caldwell skull uses 15° caudad angle.

stem: Lateral skull requires CR centered:

opts.B: 2 inches anterior and 2 inches superior to EAM

exp: Center approx. 2 inches anterior and 2 inches superior to the EAM.

stem: Waters view for facial bones requires OML forming:
exp: Waters positions the MML about 37° to IR for facial bones/maxillary sinuses.

stem: Tangential zygomatic arches require CR angled:
exp: Tangential arches commonly use ~45°.

stem: AP axial clavicle requires CR angled:
exp: AP axial clavicle uses cephalad angle to project above ribs.

stem: Sternum RAO requires patient rotated approximately:
exp: RAO sternum uses ~15–20° rotation.

stem: Lateral calcaneus includes visualization of:
exp: Lateral calcaneus demonstrates subtalar joint region.

stem: AP pelvis requires internal rotation of legs approximately:
exp: Rotate legs 10–15° internally to place femoral necks parallel to IR.

stem: AP scapula requires:
exp: Abduct the arm to move scapula laterally away from ribs.

stem: Which action best reduces the risk of a patient fall during portable radiography?

opts.A: Ensure call light is out of reach to limit movement
opts.B: Lock the bed and confirm brakes before positioning
opts.C: Have the patient sit on the bed edge unattended
opts.D: Raise the bed to standing height

exp: Falls are reduced by stabilizing the environment—locking the bed/wheels and maintaining safe positioning.

stem: A patient speaks limited English and nods to every question. What communication strategy is most appropriate?

opts.A: Use louder speech and shorter words
opts.B: Ask a child family member to translate
opts.D: Rely on gestures only to save time

exp: Qualified interpreters improve accuracy and safety; family (especially minors) may introduce errors and confidentiality issues.

stem: Which situation most strongly suggests the need for airborne precautions?
exp: Airborne isolation is used for organisms spread via droplet nuclei, such as suspected or confirmed TB.

stem: A patient becomes pale, diaphoretic, and reports nausea immediately after standing. What is the most likely cause?
exp: Sudden pallor, diaphoresis, nausea, and near-syncope are typical of a vasovagal response.

stem: A radiographer is assisting a patient with oxygen via nasal cannula. What is most important before moving the patient?

opts.A: Confirm tubing is secure and oxygen source can move with the patient
opts.B: Increase the flow rate to compensate for movement
opts.C: Remove the cannula to prevent tangling
opts.D: Switch to room air during transfer

exp: Secure tubing and ensure portable oxygen/adequate slack to avoid dislodgement and hypoxia during movement.

stem: Which item requires a sharps container for disposal?
exp: Needles are sharps and must go directly into an approved sharps container.

stem: Before transporting a patient on a stretcher, the radiographer should first:

opts.A: Verify patient identity using two identifiers
opts.B: Ask the patient to state their diagnosis

exp: Two-identifier verification is a core legal/safety step before performing or transporting for an exam.

stem: Which documentation practice is MOST appropriate after an unusual event during an exam?

opts.A: Add personal opinions about the cause
opts.B: Document only if the patient complains
opts.C: Wait until the end of the shift to chart
opts.D: Document objective facts and actions taken

exp: Clinical documentation should be timely, objective, and focused on facts and interventions.

stem: A patient with latex allergy is scheduled for an exam. The radiographer should:

opts.A: Ensure latex-free supplies and alert the team
opts.C: Cancel all imaging exams for the day
opts.D: Use standard gloves and observe for itching

exp: Latex allergy requires avoidance—use latex-free gloves/supplies and communicate precautions.

stem: Which device is specifically intended to assist safe transfers and reduce staff injury risk?
exp: A gait belt provides controlled handholds during transfers and ambulation assistance.

stem: Which interaction is most responsible for image receptor exposure in diagnostic radiography?
exp: At diagnostic energies, Compton interactions dominate and contribute most to receptor exposure and scatter.

stem: What is the unit of absorbed dose in the SI system?
exp: Gray (Gy) is the SI unit of absorbed dose (J/kg).

stem: Half-value layer (HVL) is a measure of beam:
exp: HVL reflects beam quality/penetration and is affected by kVp and filtration.

stem: The oxygen enhancement effect means radiation damage is typically:

opts.D: Only relevant at nuclear medicine energies

exp: Oxygen “fixes” free radical damage, making well-oxygenated tissues more radiosensitive than hypoxic tissues.

stem: Which factor directly increases the probability of photoelectric absorption?
exp: Photoelectric absorption increases strongly with higher Z and decreases as photon energy increases.

stem: A genetic effect from radiation exposure would be classified as:
exp: Genetic (heritable) effects are stochastic—probability increases with dose, severity does not.

stem: Which statement about linear energy transfer (LET) is true?

opts.A: LET is unrelated to biological effect
opts.C: Higher LET generally produces more dense ionization tracks
opts.D: X-rays have higher LET than neutrons

exp: High-LET radiation deposits energy densely, increasing biologic effectiveness compared with low-LET radiation.

stem: The primary source of occupational exposure in diagnostic radiography is:

opts.B: Leakage radiation from the tube housing

exp: Scatter from the patient is the main contributor to staff dose during exams.

stem: A radiographer’s personal dosimeter should be worn:

opts.A: Under the lead apron at the waist
opts.B: In a pocket on the scrub top
opts.C: On the back of the hand
opts.D: Outside the lead apron at collar level

exp: For apron-wearing procedures, the primary badge is typically worn at collar level outside the apron to monitor head/neck dose.

stem: Which practice best follows ALARA during mobile radiography?

opts.A: Remove collimation to ensure anatomy is included
opts.B: Use the shortest practical exposure time and maximize distance
opts.C: Stand as close to the patient as possible
opts.D: Use higher mAs than needed to avoid repeats

exp: Time, distance, and shielding are core ALARA principles; maximize distance and avoid repeats with proper technique and collimation.

stem: A lead apron labeled 0.5 mm Pb equivalence is MOST effective for attenuating:

opts.B: All radiation including gamma from PET

exp: Lead aprons are designed primarily to reduce diagnostic x-ray scatter to staff.

stem: Which factor MOST increases patient dose during fluoroscopy?
exp: Dose accumulates with fluoroscopy time; last image hold and collimation reduce dose, longer SSD increases dose.

stem: Which change MOST reduces scatter to personnel during fluoroscopy?

opts.D: Use protective drapes and stand on the image receptor side when appropriate

exp: Scatter is reduced by shielding (drapes) and positioning; the image receptor side typically has lower scatter than the tube side.

stem: The primary barrier in a radiographic room is designed to attenuate:
exp: Primary barriers are placed where the primary beam may strike; they must attenuate the primary beam plus scatter.

stem: Which practice is appropriate for storing personal dosimeters?

opts.A: In the car on the dashboard
opts.B: In a designated low-radiation area away from heat
opts.C: Near a radiation source for accuracy
opts.D: In the lead apron pocket between uses

exp: Badges should be stored in a controlled low-radiation area and not exposed to heat or unnecessary radiation.

stem: A common cause of a light field–radiation field misalignment is:
exp: The collimator mirror/lamp alignment affects light vs x-ray field congruence; misposition can cause misalignment.

stem: In a digital radiography QC program, the primary purpose of a reject analysis is to:

opts.A: Increase exposure factors to reduce noise
opts.B: Prove that all images are diagnostic
opts.C: Identify common causes of repeats and reduce them
opts.D: Eliminate the need for technique charts

exp: Reject analysis targets repeat trends (positioning, exposure, artifacts) to reduce patient dose and improve workflow.

stem: Which QC test is MOST directly related to verifying accurate exposure time?
exp: Timer accuracy directly measures whether the selected exposure time matches actual output.

stem: Which item is routinely inspected for cracks as part of protective apparel quality control?
exp: Lead aprons are inspected (often with fluoroscopy) for cracks/tears to ensure adequate attenuation.

stem: A CR reader error indicates plate “fogging.” The most likely cause is:

opts.D: Excessive delay between exposure and reading with the plate exposed to ambient radiation/light per policy

exp: CR plates can accumulate background signal; delayed processing increases fog and reduces contrast.

stem: Which artifact is MOST associated with a grid that is upside down (focused grid used incorrectly)?
exp: Reversing a focused grid increases cutoff, often seen as peripheral density loss.

stem: A monitor used for diagnostic interpretation should be evaluated regularly for:
exp: Display performance (luminance, grayscale) affects image perception and is part of QC for digital interpretation systems.

stem: Which QC measurement is MOST useful for evaluating beam quality changes over time?
exp: HVL reflects beam quality and will change with filtration/kVp calibration issues.

stem: A sudden increase in repeat images due to motion blur most directly suggests a problem with:
exp: Motion repeats are usually workflow/communication/immobilization issues rather than equipment beam quality issues.

stem: A DR system’s exposure index (EI) is primarily used to:

opts.D: Estimate receptor exposure and help evaluate technique selection

exp: EI is a feedback metric related to detector exposure; it helps detect over/underexposure trends.

stem: Which action is appropriate when a portable x-ray unit’s exposure switch sticks in the “on” position?

opts.A: Remove the unit from service and report it immediately

exp: A malfunctioning exposure switch is a serious safety issue; remove from service and report per policy.

stem: A technique chart is MOST useful for:

opts.A: Standardizing exposure factors to achieve consistent receptor exposure

exp: Technique charts standardize factors and reduce repeats by matching technique to part thickness/projection.

stem: Which change will MOST improve recorded spatial resolution?
exp: Reducing object-to-image distance (OID) reduces geometric unsharpness and improves recorded detail.

stem: Opening collimation beyond the anatomy of interest will MOST likely:
exp: Larger field size increases scatter production, reducing image contrast.

stem: The primary factor controlling radiographic contrast (subject contrast) is:
exp: kVp strongly affects beam penetration and differential absorption, influencing subject contrast.

stem: A radiograph shows magnification. Which factor MOST directly causes magnification?
exp: Magnification increases as the object is moved farther from the image receptor (increased OID).

stem: For an AP portable chest radiograph, the central ray is typically directed to the level of:
exp: The CR for an AP chest is generally centered to T7 (inferior angle of scapula) to include lung apices and bases.

stem: Which breathing instruction is standard for an upright PA chest radiograph?

opts.D: Expose on the second full inspiration

exp: A second full inspiration helps maximize lung inflation for evaluation of lung fields.

stem: For an AP abdomen (KUB), the CR is typically centered:
exp: A standard AP abdomen is centered at the iliac crests to include the kidneys through pubic symphysis as appropriate for KUB.

stem: A left lateral decubitus abdomen is most commonly performed to evaluate:
exp: Left lateral decubitus abdomen (horizontal beam) is used to demonstrate free air when the patient cannot stand.

stem: Which structure should be included on an upright abdomen to assess for air–fluid levels?
exp: An upright abdomen should include the diaphragm to assess for free air and air–fluid levels.

stem: For an AP portable chest, rotation is best assessed by comparing:

opts.C: Medial ends of the clavicles to the vertebral column

exp: Equal distance of medial clavicles from the spine indicates minimal rotation on an AP chest.

stem: When performing a PA chest on a patient in a wheelchair, the CR should be:

opts.C: Perpendicular to the IR and centered to T7

exp: Standard PA chest uses a perpendicular CR centered to T7; adjust positioning rather than beam angle when possible.

stem: For a lateral sternum, the exposure is typically made:

opts.C: During shallow breathing with long exposure

exp: A breathing technique blurs lung markings, improving visualization of the sternum on lateral projection.

stem: For a lateral wrist, the correct position places the wrist in:

opts.C: True lateral with radius and ulna superimposed

exp: A true lateral wrist demonstrates superimposition of the distal radius and ulna with metacarpals nearly superimposed.

stem: The PA oblique hand is commonly performed with the hand rotated:
exp: A 45° oblique positions metacarpals and phalanges to demonstrate joint spaces and reduce superimposition.

stem: Which projection best demonstrates the patella in profile?
exp: Tangential (sunrise/Merchant) views profile the patellofemoral joint and patella.

stem: Which projection best demonstrates the radial head free of superimposition on the ulna?

opts.B: AP oblique elbow with lateral rotation
opts.D: AP oblique elbow with medial rotation

exp: Lateral (external) oblique elbow demonstrates the radial head/neck with minimal ulna superimposition.

stem: For an AP ankle, the foot should be positioned in:

opts.C: Dorsiflexion with the plantar surface perpendicular to the IR

exp: AP ankle requires dorsiflexion to place malleoli parallel to the IR and reduce distortion.

stem: Which sign indicates a true lateral knee?

opts.A: Tibial plateau fully open on AP
opts.C: Patella centered between femoral condyles on AP
opts.D: Fibular head completely superimposed by tibia

exp: Superimposition of femoral condyles indicates a true lateral knee.

stem: A lateral cervical spine should include which anatomy?

opts.A: C1–C7 and the top of T1

exp: A diagnostic lateral C-spine includes C1 through C7 and ideally the cervicothoracic junction (top of T1).

stem: For an AP pelvis, internal rotation of the legs (when not contraindicated) is used to:

opts.A: Place femoral necks parallel to the IR and reduce foreshortening
opts.B: Demonstrate the lesser trochanters in profile

exp: 15–20° internal rotation places femoral necks parallel to IR, improving visualization and reducing foreshortening.

stem: On an AP axial (Fuchs) odontoid projection, the dens is projected:

opts.B: Between the superior incisors and the base of the skull

exp: Fuchs method projects the dens through the foramen magnum and between the upper incisors and base of skull region.

stem: When transferring a patient from stretcher to table, which is the BEST practice to reduce injury risk?

opts.A: Use a transfer device (e.g., slide board) and team lift with clear commands
opts.B: Pull the patient by the arms to save time
opts.C: Lift with a rounded back to keep the patient close
opts.D: Have the patient stand without assistance if they are dizzy

exp: Safe patient handling uses devices, teamwork, and neutral spine/body mechanics.

stem: Which communication strategy BEST improves understanding for a patient with limited English proficiency?

opts.A: Use medical jargon to sound authoritative
opts.B: Rely on family members to translate complex consent
opts.C: Speak louder and repeat the same sentence
opts.D: Use a qualified medical interpreter and teach-back

exp: Qualified interpreters and teach-back reduce errors and improve comprehension.

stem: A patient becomes diaphoretic and confused during an exam and reports they have diabetes. What is the MOST likely issue?
exp: Diaphoresis and confusion in a diabetic patient commonly indicate hypoglycemia; initiate emergency response per protocol.

stem: A patient reports a history of latex allergy prior to an exam. What is the MOST appropriate initial action?

opts.A: Proceed; latex allergy does not affect imaging
opts.B: Ask the patient to sign a waiver and continue
opts.C: Administer contrast to test for reaction
opts.D: Stop and notify the radiologist/ordering provider and apply latex-free precautions

exp: Latex allergy requires immediate precautions and appropriate notification before proceeding.

stem: Which x-ray production process occurs when a high-speed electron is decelerated near the nucleus of a target atom?
exp: Bremsstrahlung is produced by electron deceleration in the nuclear field.

stem: Which tissue is generally considered among the MOST radiosensitive?
exp: Rapidly dividing, less differentiated tissues (e.g., gonads, marrow) tend to be more radiosensitive.

stem: Which interaction is MOST likely at lower photon energies and is responsible for improved subject contrast in bone?
exp: Photoelectric absorption increases with higher atomic number and lower energies, contributing to contrast in bone.

stem: Which is the BEST justification for collimation?

opts.A: Allows lower SID without geometric blur
opts.B: Increases scatter to fill the detector
opts.C: Reduces patient dose and scatter, improving image quality

exp: Collimation decreases volume irradiated, reducing scatter and dose while improving contrast.

stem: For personnel monitoring, the primary purpose of a dosimeter is to:

opts.B: Reduce patient dose by automatic exposure control
opts.D: Measure occupational radiation exposure for recordkeeping and safety compliance

exp: Dosimeters monitor accumulated occupational dose and support regulatory compliance.

stem: A technologist masks off anatomy after the exposure to 'hide' a positioning error. This is BEST described as:

opts.B: A method to reduce patient dose

exp: Post-exposure cropping/masking to misrepresent anatomy is inappropriate; it may violate policy and ethics.

stem: Which statement about CR plates is TRUE?

opts.A: CR eliminates the need for QC
opts.B: Plates can retain latent images; proper erasure helps prevent ghosting
opts.D: CR has no risk of artifacts from debris

exp: CR plates can retain latent images; erasure and QC reduce artifacts/ghosting.

stem: Which system standard is MOST associated with medical image communication and interoperability?
exp: DICOM is the standard for medical image formatting and communication across systems.

stem: For an AP axial (Towne) skull projection, the central ray is directed:

opts.B: Perpendicular to the IR with no angulation
opts.C: Caudad (toward the feet) relative to the OML
opts.D: Cephalad (toward the head) relative to the OML

exp: Towne is an AP axial skull projection with caudad angulation relative to OML (commonly adjusted per protocol).

stem: For a cross-table lateral hip (trauma), the unaffected leg should be positioned:

opts.B: Extended straight and parallel to affected leg
opts.D: Elevated and flexed out of the way to avoid superimposition

exp: Cross-table lateral hip requires moving the unaffected leg out of the field to prevent superimposition.

stem: For an AP open mouth (C1-C2) projection, which structure should be visualized through the open mouth?

opts.B: Dens (odontoid) and lateral masses of C1

exp: Open mouth aims to show dens and lateral masses of C1 with minimal superimposition.

stem: A swimmer’s lateral projection is primarily used to demonstrate:
exp: Swimmer’s lateral helps visualize the cervicothoracic junction when shoulders obscure it.

stem: For an AP abdomen upright, the primary reason for the upright position is to demonstrate:

opts.B: Air-fluid levels and free intraperitoneal air

exp: Upright abdomen helps show air-fluid levels and free air under the diaphragm.

stem: Which projection is most appropriate to demonstrate pleural effusion layering?
exp: Decubitus with horizontal beam demonstrates fluid layering (dependent side).

stem: Which symptoms are consistent with a mild contrast reaction? (Select all that apply.)
exp: Mild reactions include nausea, urticaria, and warmth; respiratory arrest is severe.

stem: Which tissues are considered radiosensitive? (Select all that apply.)
exp: Rapidly dividing tissues like marrow, gonads, and GI lining are radiosensitive.

stem: Which factors directly affect spatial resolution in digital radiography? (Select all that apply.)
exp: Spatial resolution is affected by pixel size, focal spot size, and motion; kVp affects contrast more directly.

stem: During a contrast study, a patient reports itching and develops hives but has no respiratory distress. What is the best immediate action?

opts.A: Reassure the patient this is expected and continue the exam
opts.B: Give the patient water and observe without reporting
opts.C: Document after the exam and discharge the patient if stable
opts.D: Stop contrast administration and notify the radiologist promptly

exp: Urticaria suggests an allergic-like reaction; stopping contrast and notifying the radiologist allows timely evaluation and treatment. Continuing risks progression. Observation without notifying delays care. Documentation is important but not the first priority.

stem: Which statement best describes a stochastic effect of radiation?

opts.A: It occurs only in rapidly dividing tissues and has a threshold
opts.B: It has no known threshold and probability increases with dose
opts.C: It is immediate and always reversible at diagnostic doses
opts.D: It has a threshold dose and severity increases with dose

exp: Stochastic effects are modeled as having no threshold; probability rises with dose. Deterministic effects have thresholds and severity increases with dose. Tissue sensitivity varies, but that doesn’t define stochastic effects. Immediate/reversible is not a defining feature.

stem: A grid is added for an abdomen radiograph. To maintain detector exposure, which change is most appropriate?
exp: Grids absorb scatter (and some primary), so mAs typically must increase to maintain receptor exposure. Decreasing mAs underexposes. Large kVp reductions can harm penetration and may not correct grid-related exposure loss. SID changes affect geometry, not grid transmission requirements.

stem: A digital radiograph appears acceptable but shows an exposure indicator consistently higher than target across multiple patients. What is the most likely issue?

opts.A: Motion artifact due to long exposure time
opts.D: Too low kVp causing quantum mottle

exp: Repeated higher-than-target exposure indicators suggest systematically high exposure (dose creep), even if post-processing masks brightness. Grid cutoff typically lowers detector exposure. Low kVp with insufficient mAs increases noise and often looks degraded. Motion artifact is visual and not primarily reflected by elevated exposure indicators.

stem: A quality control test reveals consistent misalignment between the light field and the x-ray field. What is the primary concern?

opts.A: Unintended patient exposure outside the intended anatomy

exp: Light/x-ray field misalignment can irradiate anatomy outside the intended region and compromise collimation accuracy. Focal spot size is unrelated. Detector lag is a receptor performance issue. Interaction rates depend on beam energy and tissue, not field lamp alignment.

stem: On an AP pelvis radiograph, the right obturator foramen appears larger than the left. What does this most likely indicate?

opts.A: Patient is rotated toward the right
opts.C: Patient is rotated toward the left
opts.D: Right hip is externally rotated only

exp: The obturator foramen appears larger on the side farther from the detector; if the right foramen is larger, the patient is rotated toward the left. Hip rotation affects femoral neck appearance, not pelvic rotation symmetry.

stem: An abdominal series is ordered to evaluate possible bowel obstruction. Which positioning choice best demonstrates air-fluid levels?

opts.B: Trendelenburg position to shift gas inferiorly
opts.D: Upright abdomen (or left lateral decubitus if unable to stand)

exp: Upright or decubitus positioning allows air-fluid levels to form and be visualized. Supine/prone alone may miss levels. Trendelenburg is not standard for demonstrating obstruction air-fluid levels.

stem: Half-value layer (HVL) is most useful for describing:
exp: HVL quantifies how much material is needed to reduce beam intensity by half and is used as an indicator of beam quality. It does not directly measure detector exposure, image contrast, or scatter dose.

stem: A grid is used for a large patient. Which consequence is most expected if exposure factors are not adjusted?
exp: Grids absorb scatter and some primary radiation; without compensating mAs, receptor exposure drops. Magnification is geometric. Scatter production depends on tissue volume and kVp; the grid removes scatter reaching the detector but doesn’t reduce scatter created.

stem: A digital image appears overly bright after processing, but the exposure indicator suggests underexposure. Which is the most likely cause?

opts.B: Over-collimation or improper field recognition affecting histogram analysis

exp: Incorrect field recognition/histogram analysis can produce misleading brightness despite low exposure indicator. Excessive mAs typically increases exposure indicator. Grid cutoff decreases exposure and tends to produce noisy images. Higher kVp usually increases scatter unless controlled.

stem: Detective quantum efficiency (DQE) is best described as a detector’s ability to:

opts.A: Convert x-ray signal into an image with minimal noise for a given exposure
opts.B: Increase patient dose to reduce noise

exp: DQE reflects how efficiently a detector produces image quality for a given exposure. It does not justify increasing dose, replace collimation, or address motion blur.

stem: A department’s repeat analysis shows frequent repeats due to “underexposure” even though techniques are unchanged. Which equipment issue is most plausible?

opts.B: Increased patient thickness across all exams
opts.C: Increased SID in the room without documentation
opts.D: Decreased detector sensitivity or calibration drift

exp: Calibration drift or sensitivity changes can cause systematic exposure problems without technique changes. SID changes would be noticed operationally and are not typically uniform across all work. Patient thickness wouldn’t shift globally across all exams. Collimation doesn’t cause underexposure by itself.

stem: On a PA chest radiograph, the right sternoclavicular joint appears farther from the spine than the left. This most likely indicates the patient was rotated:
exp: If the right SC joint is farther from the spine, the right side is farther from the detector, indicating rotation toward the right. Shoulder elevation affects clavicle position, not SC symmetry relative to the spine.

stem: For an AP supine abdomen intended to include the symphysis pubis, the CR is centered:
exp: Centering about 2 inches above the iliac crest helps include the lower abdomen and symphysis pubis. Centering at the crest often excludes the pubis. Xiphoid is too high. Symphysis is too low for routine abdomen coverage.

stem: On a lateral knee radiograph, the femoral condyles are not superimposed, and the medial condyle appears posterior. What correction is most appropriate?

opts.B: Rotate the knee slightly away from the affected side (increase external rotation)
opts.C: Rotate the knee slightly toward the affected side (increase internal rotation)

exp: If the medial condyle is posterior, the knee is rotated too externally; correcting requires slight internal rotation to superimpose condyles. Flexion affects patellofemoral relationship but not condylar superimposition. SID changes don’t correct rotation.

stem: A young child is crying and resisting positioning for a chest radiograph. What is the best initial strategy?

opts.A: Restrain the child immediately to prevent repeats
opts.B: Delay the exam until the end of the day
opts.C: Use age-appropriate explanation and enlist a parent/guardian to comfort and assist per policy
opts.D: Proceed without immobilization and take multiple quick exposures

exp: Calm, age-appropriate coaching and appropriate guardian assistance improves cooperation and reduces repeats. Immediate restraint may escalate distress and should follow policy and necessity, not be first-line. Delaying may not be clinically appropriate. Multiple exposures increases dose.

stem: For an AP hip, internal rotation of the affected leg is primarily used to:

opts.C: Place the femoral neck parallel to the detector

exp: Internal rotation corrects natural femoral anteversion, placing the femoral neck more parallel to the detector and reducing foreshortening. Rotation doesn’t directly reduce dose. SI joints and acetabulum profile require different positioning.

stem: After IV contrast injection, a patient develops swelling and tightness at the IV site. This finding most likely indicates:
exp: Local swelling and tightness at the injection site are classic for contrast extravasation/infiltration.

stem: A patient reports a prior reaction to iodinated contrast. Which action is most appropriate before scheduling a contrast exam?

opts.A: Schedule the exam at the end of the day
opts.B: Confirm reaction details and notify provider for possible premedication plan
opts.C: Ignore the history if the reaction was mild

exp: A prior reaction requires assessment and provider involvement (e.g., alternative contrast, premedication, or different study).

stem: Which instruction is MOST appropriate for a patient after a barium enema?

opts.A: Remain NPO until the next day
opts.B: Take insulin even if not eating
opts.C: Expect white stools and increase fluids as directed

exp: Barium can lighten stool; hydration helps prevent constipation/impaction per facility guidance.

stem: A diabetic patient becomes confused and sweaty during an exam. What is the radiographer’s best immediate response?

opts.C: Check for a medical alert and call for assistance per protocol

exp: Symptoms suggest hypoglycemia; stop the exam and initiate emergency response per facility protocol.

stem: Increasing filtration primarily affects the x-ray beam by:

opts.A: Increasing photon quantity without changing energy
opts.B: Removing low-energy photons and increasing average energy
opts.C: Decreasing average energy by removing high-energy photons
opts.D: Increasing scatter production in the patient

exp: Filtration preferentially removes low-energy photons, hardening the beam (higher average energy) and reducing skin dose.

stem: Which tissue is generally considered most radiosensitive?
exp: Rapidly dividing, less-differentiated tissues such as bone marrow are more radiosensitive.

stem: If mA is doubled and exposure time is halved, the primary effect on patient exposure is:
exp: mAs (mA × time) remains constant, so quantity of radiation (and patient exposure) is approximately unchanged.

stem: Increasing kVp while keeping mAs constant generally causes patient dose to:

opts.A: Become zero due to higher penetration
opts.B: Increase markedly because photons are more penetrating
opts.D: Decrease if mAs is reduced appropriately (15% rule concept)

exp: Higher kVp increases penetration; if kVp is increased and mAs reduced to maintain receptor exposure, patient dose can decrease.

stem: In fluoroscopy, which change MOST reduces patient entrance skin dose rate?

opts.C: Use pulsed fluoroscopy with lower pulse rate
opts.D: Increase field of view by opening collimation

exp: Pulsed fluoroscopy (lower pulse rate) reduces dose rate; magnification and shorter SSD increase dose.

stem: The main purpose of collimation is to:

opts.A: Reduce patient dose and scatter radiation
opts.C: Increase image contrast by increasing scatter

exp: Beam restriction reduces irradiated volume, lowering patient dose and scatter (improving contrast).

stem: A pregnant radiographer should:

opts.A: Wear no dosimeter to avoid anxiety
opts.B: Declare pregnancy in writing if they choose, to enable fetal monitoring practices

exp: Declaration is voluntary; written declaration allows implementation of fetal dose monitoring and work practice adjustments.

stem: When performing portable radiography on a ventilated ICU patient, the best staff protection is to:

opts.A: Have staff step back at least 6 feet if possible and use shielding when available
opts.B: Increase kVp to shorten exposure time
opts.C: Ask all staff to hold the patient
opts.D: Turn the bed sideways toward the tube

exp: Distance is highly effective; stepping back reduces scatter exposure, and shielding adds additional protection.

stem: To minimize fetal dose during a necessary abdominal radiograph of a pregnant patient, the radiographer should:

opts.A: Use gridless technique regardless of size
opts.B: Increase field size to include more anatomy
opts.C: Repeat the exposure to ensure no motion
opts.D: Use tight collimation and proper shielding when it does not obscure anatomy

exp: Dose reduction focuses on avoiding repeats, tight collimation, and shielding when appropriate without compromising the exam.

stem: A technologist notices a consistent “dead pixel” line on multiple DR images. The best action is to:

opts.A: Increase mAs to cover the defect
opts.B: Ignore it if anatomy is visible
opts.C: Report it for detector calibration/service evaluation
opts.D: Switch to a grid for every exam

exp: Persistent pixel defects indicate a detector/calibration issue that requires QC or service, not exposure increases.

stem: If an AEC system consistently produces images that are too dark, the MOST appropriate first step is to:

opts.A: Check correct chamber selection and positioning over the anatomy of interest

exp: AEC performance depends on correct chamber selection and anatomy alignment; mis-centering commonly causes density errors.

stem: The backup timer on an AEC-equipped unit primarily prevents:

opts.B: Overexposure if the AEC fails to terminate the exposure

exp: Backup timers limit maximum exposure time if AEC does not terminate, preventing excessive patient dose.

stem: A consistent “moire” pattern on CR images is most commonly caused by:
